content.problemStatement:

For a positive integer sss, let EsE_{s}Esâ€‹ be the expected number of lucky arrays AAA such that âˆ‘i=1NAi=s\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{i}=sâˆ‘i=1Nâ€‹Aiâ€‹=s.

It can be shown that the answer can be represented as PQ\frac{P}{Q}QPâ€‹, where PPP and QQQ are coprime integers and Qâ‰¡Ì¸0(mod109+7)Q \not\equiv 0 \pmod{10^9+7}Qî€ â‰¡0(mod109+7). Print the value of Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1(mod109+7)P \cdot Q^{-1} \pmod{10^9+7}Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1(mod109+7).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
First and only line of each test contains 555 space-separated integers N,M,K,X,YN, M, K, X, YN,M,K,X,Y, as mentioned in the statement.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the value âˆ‘s=1KEs\sum_{s=1}^{K} E_{s}âˆ‘s=1Kâ€‹Esâ€‹.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2â‹…1052\cdot 10^52â‹…105.

The sum of KKK over all test cases won't exceed 2â‹…1052\cdot 10^52â‹…105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array AAA has size 111, and matrix BBB has size 1Ã—11\times 11Ã—1.
Probability of B(i,j)=1B_{(i,j)} = 1B(i,j)â€‹=1 is 12\frac{1}{2}21â€‹. Therefore, the expected number of lucky arrays for s=1s = 1s=1 is 12=500000004modâ€‰â€‰(109+7)\frac{1}{2} = 500000004 \mod (10^9 + 7)21â€‹=500000004mod(109+7).
For  s=2s = 2s=2, the expected number of lucky arrays is 000 because the sum can never be 222.
Hence, the final answer is 500000004500000004500000004.
